title: Willow square base
subtitle: Setting up and weaving a rectangular willow base for shopping baskets and trays
excerpt: The square or rectangular base is the alternative to the round slath base: parallel stakes crossed at right angles and paired off to produce a flat, stable base for shopping baskets, garden trugs, and trays. The construction is more predictable than the round base for beginners because the staking direction is fixed rather than radial.
sourceNotes: Traditional British stake-and-strand basketwork; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for square-base construction and randing stitch method.

content.text: A square willow base works on a simple grid: one set of
stakes

runs in one direction, a second set crosses at right angles, and the weaving rods lock them together. This is the base construction for a shopping basket (an oval or rectangular form), a garden trug, or a flat display tray. It is more predictable than the round slath base because the stakes maintain a fixed parallel arrangement rather than the radiating geometry of a round base.
What you need

For a 35 by 22 cm base (a standard shopping basket size): 5 long stakes at 55 cm (the length direction), 7 short stakes at 42 cm (the width direction), all 7 to 8 mm diameter. Weavers: 16 fine rods of 4 to 5 mm diameter, at least 90 cm long. Soak all rods for the appropriate time for the willow type before starting.
Step-by-step

Lay the 5 long stakes parallel on the work surface, 5 cm apart. Use the bodkin to pierce through the centre of each long stake, creating a slot running lengthways through it. Thread all 7 short stakes through the slots so they cross the long stakes at right angles. The crossings should form an even grid at the centre of all the stakes.
Work 4 rows of
pairing

across the short stakes, working from the centre of the base outward in both directions. On the first 2 rows, treat each long stake as a single unit; do not separate them. After 2 rows, work each long stake individually.
Switch to
randing

for the main body of the base: one rod passing over one stake, under the next, across the full width. Randing builds the base surface faster than pairing. Use randing for 4 to 6 rows on each side of the centre pairing section.

Finish the base with 2 further rows of pairing at each end to consolidate the edge before staking up the sides. The edge pairing rows prevent the randing from spreading when the stakes are bent upward.

Trim the weaver ends close on the underside of the base with the sloyd knife. Check that the base lies flat; any bow in the base should be corrected by pressing the base onto a flat surface before the rods dry fully. The base should hold its flat shape without weights once the willow has set, which takes 2 to 3 hours in a warm room.
What a correct base looks like

A correctly woven square base lies completely flat, has an even pairing band on each short end and consistent randing rows across the middle, and shows clean, equal spacing between all the long stakes. The short stakes protrude an equal amount on each side for later insertion into the basket sides. The weave should be dense enough that it cannot be pulled apart by hand at any point; if any row feels loose, re-work it while the willow is still wet.
Common mistakes
Slots not running parallel to the long axis: the short stakes will not sit at 90 degrees to the long stakes if the slots are pierced at an angle. Check with a set square before threading through.

Starting randing before the centre pairing is complete: randing without the anchor of pairing at the centre will slide along the stakes rather than locking in place. Complete 4 rows of pairing at the centre before switching to randing.

Uneven spacing between long stakes: the randing rows will be tighter in some sections than others, making the base surface uneven. Measure the spacing with a ruler before working the first row and re-space if needed.
